content: Accurate measurement of incidence requires: (1) clear case definition with standardized diagnostic criteria; (2) defined population at risk (excluding those with prior disease); (3) complete case ascertainment through surveillance systems or active case finding; (4) accurate denominator data (person-time at risk). Common biases include: under-ascertainment (missing cases), especially for asymptomatic conditions; changes in diagnostic criteria over time artificially altering incidence trends; migration affecting denominator accuracy. Prevalence measurement requires: (1) representative sampling or complete population enumeration; (2) valid diagnostic tools applied consistently; (3) timing specification (point prevalence vs period prevalence). Biases include: survival bias (prevalent cases are survivors, not incident cases); recall bias in self-reported surveys; non-response bias if diseased individuals are less likely to participate. Period prevalence (cases existing during a time interval) exceeds point prevalence and approximates incidence for short-duration diseases. Administrative data (ICD codes) often underestimate true incidence/prevalence due to coding errors and incomplete capture of undiagnosed cases.
